title: DOL model COBRA notices needed a refresh by Jan 31, 2026
body: The Department of Labor's model COBRA election and general notices carried an expiration date of January 31, 2026. Using an expired model past that date forfeits the “good faith compliance” safe harbor that comes from using the model language as-is — worth a one-time sweep to confirm every active template has actually been migrated.

sources.title: DOL — An Employer's Guide to COBRA

title: Penalty exposure is bigger than most people assume
body: DOL can assess up to $110/day/participant for missed COBRA notices. The IRS adds a parallel excise tax — $100/day per beneficiary, or $200/day per family — and if non-compliance surfaces during an IRS audit uncorrected, the minimum penalty is $2,500 per beneficiary, scaling to $15,000 per violation if it's judged “more than trivial,” capped annually at the lesser of $500,000 or 10% of the prior year's health costs.

title: Multi-state complexity is the norm, not the exception
body: 44 states now maintain some form of their own continuation-coverage (“mini-COBRA”) law. A TPA operating across the DMV region alone is running at least four separate rulebooks — federal plus Maryland, D.C., and Virginia — per client roster.

title: TPA / claims-services consolidation is accelerating
body: The insurance TPA market is valued around $592B in 2026 and projected to reach roughly $845B by 2031 (~7.4% CAGR), and regional or specialty firms are increasingly being acquired by larger platforms chasing scale, geographic reach, and broader service offerings.

title: AI has moved from “emerging” to core infrastructure
body: Automation and AI adoption inside benefits administration are accelerating fast, aimed at cutting administrative cost while improving accuracy and the member experience. Cloud platforms and generative AI are now considered mainstream tooling rather than experimental add-ons.

title: The TPA value proposition is shifting from processor to advisor
body: Employers increasingly expect TPAs to act as strategic advisors delivering actionable insight, not just administrative processing. Being the person who can explain why a rule applies — not just execute it — is directly aligned with where the industry is headed.

title: Self-funded vs. insured is the single fact that decides everything downstream
body: Before any qualifying event happens, knowing whether a plan is insured or self-funded (ASO) — confirmed from the actual administration agreement, not the sales quote — determines which law applies at all. State mini-COBRA laws can't reach self-funded plans regardless of group size.

title: D.C.'s 3-month continuation surprises people
body: D.C.'s mini-COBRA runs just 3 months — dramatically shorter than Maryland's 18 or Virginia's 12. Clients with a small D.C. office often assume it matches their Maryland headquarters' duration; flagging this proactively avoids an uncomfortable conversation later.

title: Documentation beats memory in an audit
body: The gap between a $2,500 minimum penalty and a $15,000 “more than trivial” penalty often comes down to whether the notice trail is documented and timestamped, or reconstructed from memory after the fact.
